title: Scott's Conjecture (Problem 4.1)
statement_text: If $m = \binom{n}{k}$, complete $k$-graphs have the smallest max-$r$-cut among all $m$-edge $k$-graphs.
context_text: Scott proposed this conjecture for $r = 2$ as a natural generalisation of the graph case, where complete graphs are known to be extremal for the Edwards bound on max-2-cut excess. As a corollary of Theorem 3.1, it would imply a lower bound of $\Omega(m^{(k-1)/k})$ on the maximum excess of an $m$-edge $k$-graph.
paper_contribution: Disproves the conjecture for all $k \geq 3$: for $k = 3$, Theorem 1.1 shows that Steiner triple systems are extremal (not complete graphs); for $k > 3$, Theorem 1.3 shows that in the binomial random $k$-graph $G^k(n, n^{3-k})$, a.a.s. every $r$-cut has excess at most $O(n^2) = O(m^{2/3})$, which is smaller than the complete $k$-graph bound.
notes: Referenced as [31, Problem 4.1]; publication year of [31] not recoverable from extracted text.

title: Θ(√m) excess conjecture for hypergraph cuts
statement_text: For any fixed $2 \leq r \leq k$, the smallest maximum $r$-cut over all $m$-edge $k$-graphs has excess $\Theta(\sqrt{m})$.
context_text: The authors describe this as a 'second plausible conjecture', motivated by: chromatic-number methods giving $\Omega(\sqrt{m})$ lower bounds; the standard deviation of a random cut in a linear $k$-graph being $\Theta(\sqrt{m})$; and the bound being tight for $r=k=2$ and for $r=2$, $k=3$. The paper's main Theorem 1.2 disproves this for all cases with $r \geq 3$ or $k \geq 4$, showing the excess is in fact $\Omega(m^{5/9})$ in those cases.
notes: Stated in the introduction as a 'natural guess' without a labelled environment; largely disproved by Theorem 1.2 of this same paper — the conjecture holds only for $r=2$, $k \in \{2,3\}$.
